**Session 2022-23**

chunks.text:
Section heading: Online Safety Bill

**Written evidence submitted by End Violence Against Women Coalition** **(OSB63)**

**to the Online Safety Bill Public Bill Committee**

**Introduction**

1.End Violence Against Women coalition (EVAW) is a leading coalition of over 120 specialist women’s support services, NGOs, researchers, activists and survivors and working to end violence against women and girls (VAWG) in all its forms.

2.We make this submission to the Public Bill Committee not to provide an overall commentary on the Online Safety Bill, but to specifically highlight its shortcomings in addressing online violence against women and girls. This legislation represents an important opportunity to prevent and address VAWG through a systems-based approach, however its impact will be significantly curtailed by the failure to name VAWG on the face of the Bill, within a broader human rights and equalities framework.
chunks.display_text:
Section heading: Online Safety Bill

**Written evidence submitted by End Violence Against Women Coalition** **(OSB63)**

**to the Online Safety Bill Public Bill Committee**

**Introduction**

1.End Violence Against Women coalition (EVAW) is a leading coalition of over 120 specialist women’s support services, NGOs, researchers, activists and survivors and working to end violence against women and girls (VAWG) in all its forms.

2.We make this submission to the Public Bill Committee not to provide an overall commentary on the Online Safety Bill, but to specifically highlight its shortcomings in addressing online violence against women and girls. This legislation represents an important opportunity to prevent and address VAWG through a systems-based approach, however its impact will be significantly curtailed by the failure to name VAWG on the face of the Bill, within a broader human rights and equalities framework.

chunks.text: in addressing online violence against women and girls. This legislation represents an important opportunity to prevent and address VAWG through a systems-based approach, however its impact will be significantly curtailed by the failure to name VAWG on the face of the Bill, within a broader human rights and equalities framework. 3.Overall, the Bill is misguided in adopting a gender-neutral approach, in which VAWG is likely to be addressed only in a narrow and limiting way, guided by the arbitrary selection of Schedule 7 offences and other illegal content. This approach significantly reduces the requirements placed on tech companies, in that they will only be responsible for addressing certain forms of VAWG which reach a criminal threshold. The Bill, as drafted, permits tech companies to continue to evade accountability for the ways in which the decisions they make about the design and operation of their platforms can engender VAWG in all its forms, including those not captured by criminal law. EVAW has worked with a coalition of VAWG sector and children’s experts to produce a VAWG Code of Practice which, if introduced, would mitigate this risk (see paragraph 16). The Bill’s limited provision for media literacy and absence of funding models for specialist support services are also major omissions in reducing the prevalence of online violence against women and girls, which require redress. 4.In order to ensure the Online Safety Bill adequately addresses violence against women and girls, our key recommendations are as follows: · Amend the Bill to include the Istanbul Convention definition of VAWG on the face of Bill, within an overarching human rights and equalities framework · Amend Clause 37 of the Bill to introduce a VAWG Code of Practice · Amend the Bill to introduce greater media literacy requirements
chunks.display_text: 3.Overall, the Bill is misguided in adopting a gender-neutral approach, in which VAWG is likely to be addressed only in a narrow and limiting way, guided by the arbitrary selection of Schedule 7 offences and other illegal content. This approach significantly reduces the requirements placed on tech companies, in that they will only be responsible for addressing certain forms of VAWG which reach a criminal threshold. The Bill, as drafted, permits tech companies to continue to evade accountability for the ways in which the decisions they make about the design and operation of their platforms can engender VAWG in all its forms, including those not captured by criminal law. EVAW has worked with a coalition of VAWG sector and children’s experts to produce a VAWG Code of Practice which, if introduced, would mitigate this risk (see paragraph 16). The Bill’s limited provision for media literacy and absence of funding models for specialist support services are also major omissions in reducing the prevalence of online violence against women and girls, which require redress. 4.In order to ensure the Online Safety Bill adequately addresses violence against women and girls, our key recommendations are as follows: · Amend the Bill to include the Istanbul Convention definition of VAWG on the face of Bill, within an overarching human rights and equalities framework · Amend Clause 37 of the Bill to introduce a VAWG Code of Practice · Amend the Bill to introduce greater media literacy requirements

chunks.text: follows: · Amend the Bill to include the Istanbul Convention definition of VAWG on the face of Bill, within an overarching human rights and equalities framework · Amend Clause 37 of the Bill to introduce a VAWG Code of Practice · Amend the Bill to introduce greater media literacy requirements · Provide that 10% of the Digital Services Tax are directed to funding for specialist support services **Online violence against women and girls: the case for change** 5.Online violence against women and girls, like all forms of gender-based violence, is a cause and consequence of gender inequality. 1 in 5 women have been subject to online harassment or abuse,\[1\] whilst online-facilitated child sexual abuse and grooming offences disproportionately impact girls.\[2\] Online VAWG also intersects with other forms of structural inequality, for example research suggests that Black women for example are 84% more likely to be harassed on certain platforms.\[3\] Research by EVAW and Glitch also found that Black and minoritised women and non-binary people reported higher levels of online abuse during the pandemic.\[4\] 6.Online VAWG has a significant impact on women and girls, which can include mental, physical and psychological trauma. The effects can be long lasting and wide-ranging and have been described as "shattering lives". It must be understood as part of a wider continuum of violence against women and girls, with the online space also providing a context for VAWG carried out offline. Research by Women’s Aid found for example, found that 85% of women who experienced online abuse from a partner or ex-partner said that it was part of the pattern of abuse they also experienced offline.\[5\] The online world also creates opportunities for new forms of VAWG, such as intimate image-based abuse, nudification and deepfake pornography.
chunks.display_text: · Provide that 10% of the Digital Services Tax are directed to funding for specialist support services **Online violence against women and girls: the case for change** 5.Online violence against women and girls, like all forms of gender-based violence, is a cause and consequence of gender inequality. 1 in 5 women have been subject to online harassment or abuse,\[1\] whilst online-facilitated child sexual abuse and grooming offences disproportionately impact girls.\[2\] Online VAWG also intersects with other forms of structural inequality, for example research suggests that Black women for example are 84% more likely to be harassed on certain platforms.\[3\] Research by EVAW and Glitch also found that Black and minoritised women and non-binary people reported higher levels of online abuse during the pandemic.\[4\] 6.Online VAWG has a significant impact on women and girls, which can include mental, physical and psychological trauma. The effects can be long lasting and wide-ranging and have been described as "shattering lives". It must be understood as part of a wider continuum of violence against women and girls, with the online space also providing a context for VAWG carried out offline. Research by Women’s Aid found for example, found that 85% of women who experienced online abuse from a partner or ex-partner said that it was part of the pattern of abuse they also experienced offline.\[5\] The online world also creates opportunities for new forms of VAWG, such as intimate image-based abuse, nudification and deepfake pornography.

chunks.text: for example, found that 85% of women who experienced online abuse from a partner or ex-partner said that it was part of the pattern of abuse they also experienced offline.\[5\] The online world also creates opportunities for new forms of VAWG, such as intimate image-based abuse, nudification and deepfake pornography. 7.Moreover, online VAWG has significant wider societal impacts, as women and girls are commonly compelled to, or directed to ‘come offline’, to censor and shrink themselves to avoid harm, mirroring the way in which women and girls are often directed to take responsibility for ‘keeping themselves’ safe in the offline world. In practice, this withdrawal leads to loss of opportunities for women and girls such as access to education, work, communities, support and information; both online and offline. This then impacts on public discourse and our democracy, shaping who gets to be part of what conversations and the communities that form online. Ultimately, online VAWG has a silencing effect on women and girls, violating their participatory rights and curtailing their freedom of expression. 8.At the same time, online VAWG signals the flourishing of harmful social norms, attitudes and behaviours towards women and girls, and is often indicative of a climate of impunity which sustains gender inequality. Some of the most extreme examples relate to so-called incel culture and ‘man-o-spheres’, where boys and men are radicalised online in environments where sexism and misogyny flourish. 9.We know that there is significant public appetite for the Online Safety Bill to address online VAWG. EVAW commissioned a survey in 2021, in which 74% of respondents said the government should do more to ensure social media platforms address online VAWG. A joint petition with Glitch calling for the Bill to address violence against women and girls has amassed over 60,000 signatures.
chunks.display_text: 7.Moreover, online VAWG has significant wider societal impacts, as women and girls are commonly compelled to, or directed to ‘come offline’, to censor and shrink themselves to avoid harm, mirroring the way in which women and girls are often directed to take responsibility for ‘keeping themselves’ safe in the offline world. In practice, this withdrawal leads to loss of opportunities for women and girls such as access to education, work, communities, support and information; both online and offline. This then impacts on public discourse and our democracy, shaping who gets to be part of what conversations and the communities that form online. Ultimately, online VAWG has a silencing effect on women and girls, violating their participatory rights and curtailing their freedom of expression. 8.At the same time, online VAWG signals the flourishing of harmful social norms, attitudes and behaviours towards women and girls, and is often indicative of a climate of impunity which sustains gender inequality. Some of the most extreme examples relate to so-called incel culture and ‘man-o-spheres’, where boys and men are radicalised online in environments where sexism and misogyny flourish. 9.We know that there is significant public appetite for the Online Safety Bill to address online VAWG. EVAW commissioned a survey in 2021, in which 74% of respondents said the government should do more to ensure social media platforms address online VAWG. A joint petition with Glitch calling for the Bill to address violence against women and girls has amassed over 60,000 signatures.

chunks.text: to address online VAWG. EVAW commissioned a survey in 2021, in which 74% of respondents said the government should do more to ensure social media platforms address online VAWG. A joint petition with Glitch calling for the Bill to address violence against women and girls has amassed over 60,000 signatures. 10.Finally, it is important to state that whilst tech companies themselves may not readily divulge their competing interests, online VAWG will often provide a source of engagement and profit for online services and platforms. A business model which relies on prolonged engagement, virality and advertising revenue for example, will often take decisions which are conducive to the amplification and normalisation of VAWG. The Online Safety Bill provides the government with an opportunity to communicate to such platforms what alternative obligations it must consider outside of the pursuit of profit, including duties to prevent the violation of women and girls’ rights – but it has not yet done so. The Digital, Culture, Media and Sports Committee, in its report on the draft Bill, referred to VAWG as a "key omission" of the Bill,\[6\] which we believe persists in the latest iteration. **The Online Safety Bill and violence against women and girls** ***VAWG and human rights***
chunks.display_text: 10.Finally, it is important to state that whilst tech companies themselves may not readily divulge their competing interests, online VAWG will often provide a source of engagement and profit for online services and platforms. A business model which relies on prolonged engagement, virality and advertising revenue for example, will often take decisions which are conducive to the amplification and normalisation of VAWG. The Online Safety Bill provides the government with an opportunity to communicate to such platforms what alternative obligations it must consider outside of the pursuit of profit, including duties to prevent the violation of women and girls’ rights – but it has not yet done so. The Digital, Culture, Media and Sports Committee, in its report on the draft Bill, referred to VAWG as a "key omission" of the Bill,\[6\] which we believe persists in the latest iteration. **The Online Safety Bill and violence against women and girls** ***VAWG and human rights***

chunks.text: not yet done so. The Digital, Culture, Media and Sports Committee, in its report on the draft Bill, referred to VAWG as a "key omission" of the Bill,\[6\] which we believe persists in the latest iteration. **The Online Safety Bill and violence against women and girls** ***VAWG and human rights*** 11.We strongly recommend that violence against women and girls is named on the face of the Online Safety Bill, tied to an overarching human rights and equalities framework. This should draw on the definition of VAWG in the Istanbul Convention,\[7\] the gold standard treaty for combating gender-based violence which the government has recently agreed to ratify. As it stands, there is currently no reference at all to women, girls, gender or sex in the current version of the Bill. Instead, we are informed that VAWG is addressed primarily through the choice of priority illegal content in Schedule 7 (which includes harassment, stalking and intimate image abuse for example). For these particular Schedule 7 offences, which were identified without consultation with VAWG organisations, regulated services will be required to carry out "illegal content risk assessments" in order to tackle this content, with Ofcom issuing guidance to help service providers carry these out.
chunks.display_text: 11.We strongly recommend that violence against women and girls is named on the face of the Online Safety Bill, tied to an overarching human rights and equalities framework. This should draw on the definition of VAWG in the Istanbul Convention,\[7\] the gold standard treaty for combating gender-based violence which the government has recently agreed to ratify. As it stands, there is currently no reference at all to women, girls, gender or sex in the current version of the Bill. Instead, we are informed that VAWG is addressed primarily through the choice of priority illegal content in Schedule 7 (which includes harassment, stalking and intimate image abuse for example). For these particular Schedule 7 offences, which were identified without consultation with VAWG organisations, regulated services will be required to carry out "illegal content risk assessments" in order to tackle this content, with Ofcom issuing guidance to help service providers carry these out.

chunks.text: stalking and intimate image abuse for example). For these particular Schedule 7 offences, which were identified without consultation with VAWG organisations, regulated services will be required to carry out "illegal content risk assessments" in order to tackle this content, with Ofcom issuing guidance to help service providers carry these out. 12.VAWG however, is a continuum which encompasses an incredibly wide range of practices and behaviours, which are also reinforced through harmful norms and attitudes. VAWG in its broad manifestation is not sufficiently captured by the Schedule 7 list. In the absence of VAWG being named on the Bill, the expectations placed on tech companies to prevent VAWG and the extent to which they can be held accountable, will be narrowly tied to the list of specific criminal behaviours set out in Schedule 7**,** and other illegal content. Professor Lorna Woods, in her evidence to the Committee, recommended an overarching risk assessment, as opposed to the current "circular mode" of risk assessments which are tied to "waiting for \[particular forms of\] content to be created".\[8\] 13.For the forms of VAWG that fall outside of Schedule 7, some of which might fall into content that is harmful to adults and to children, only Category 1 companies (as yet, undefined) will need to take steps to address this. We see the requirements here as relatively vague (i.e. for services to summarise the findings of their latest adults’ risk assessment and how they intend to deal with this content in their terms of service). In our experience, victims and survivors that report abuse are regularly informed that such actions do not violate platforms’ terms of service.
chunks.display_text: 12.VAWG however, is a continuum which encompasses an incredibly wide range of practices and behaviours, which are also reinforced through harmful norms and attitudes. VAWG in its broad manifestation is not sufficiently captured by the Schedule 7 list. In the absence of VAWG being named on the Bill, the expectations placed on tech companies to prevent VAWG and the extent to which they can be held accountable, will be narrowly tied to the list of specific criminal behaviours set out in Schedule 7**,** and other illegal content. Professor Lorna Woods, in her evidence to the Committee, recommended an overarching risk assessment, as opposed to the current "circular mode" of risk assessments which are tied to "waiting for \[particular forms of\] content to be created".\[8\] 13.For the forms of VAWG that fall outside of Schedule 7, some of which might fall into content that is harmful to adults and to children, only Category 1 companies (as yet, undefined) will need to take steps to address this. We see the requirements here as relatively vague (i.e. for services to summarise the findings of their latest adults’ risk assessment and how they intend to deal with this content in their terms of service). In our experience, victims and survivors that report abuse are regularly informed that such actions do not violate platforms’ terms of service.

chunks.text: vague (i.e. for services to summarise the findings of their latest adults’ risk assessment and how they intend to deal with this content in their terms of service). In our experience, victims and survivors that report abuse are regularly informed that such actions do not violate platforms’ terms of service. 14.From a principled perspective, the Bill’s current approach to addressing VAWG creates a hierarchy of harm whereby particular VAWG offences are listed at the top as ‘the most serious’ or harmful. This contrasts with long-standing evidence and best practice regarding how to assess and address VAWG. Rather than a hierarchy, we know that the impact of VAWG can only be properly understood through an understanding of the context including for example, the relationship between abuser and victim, the frequency of harm, how widely it reaches, how many people are involved, and dynamics of power and control. To provide a simple example, a photograph of a cat, or a place of employment in and of itself might not be considered harmful or a form of VAWG - and likely wouldn’t be considered a Schedule 7 offence. However, in the context of a woman who has fled a violent ex-partner and who has moved location following death threats, receiving an image of her cat or her place of work from an ex-partner would represent immense danger. Placing the most rigorous requirements on platforms to prevent *only* Schedule 7 offences and other illegal content, rather than VAWG in its broad manifestation, will severely limit the Bill’s intended impact.
chunks.display_text: 14.From a principled perspective, the Bill’s current approach to addressing VAWG creates a hierarchy of harm whereby particular VAWG offences are listed at the top as ‘the most serious’ or harmful. This contrasts with long-standing evidence and best practice regarding how to assess and address VAWG. Rather than a hierarchy, we know that the impact of VAWG can only be properly understood through an understanding of the context including for example, the relationship between abuser and victim, the frequency of harm, how widely it reaches, how many people are involved, and dynamics of power and control. To provide a simple example, a photograph of a cat, or a place of employment in and of itself might not be considered harmful or a form of VAWG - and likely wouldn’t be considered a Schedule 7 offence. However, in the context of a woman who has fled a violent ex-partner and who has moved location following death threats, receiving an image of her cat or her place of work from an ex-partner would represent immense danger. Placing the most rigorous requirements on platforms to prevent *only* Schedule 7 offences and other illegal content, rather than VAWG in its broad manifestation, will severely limit the Bill’s intended impact.

chunks.text: threats, receiving an image of her cat or her place of work from an ex-partner would represent immense danger. Placing the most rigorous requirements on platforms to prevent *only* Schedule 7 offences and other illegal content, rather than VAWG in its broad manifestation, will severely limit the Bill’s intended impact. 15.Furthermore, the Bill wrongly adopts a gender-neutral approach. Risk assessments will not be explicitly required to reflect the gendered nature of VAWG in the same way that there is in the Home Office’s Tackling VAWG strategy or its Domestic Abuse Plan. This is because the risk assessment duties for adults and children never actually name gender as a factor, nor any protected characteristics specifically - only "content that particularly affects individuals with a certain characteristic or members of a certain group." This is not defined,\[9\] and we are not confident that tech companies will make suitable judgements regarding how this should be interpreted, particularly given concerns about their lack of diversity and VAWG expertise.\[10\] A gender-neutral approach to tackling online VAWG is against **t** he advice of the gold-standard Istanbul Convention, to which the UK is a signatory and has recently agreed to ratify. Our concerns also chime with the recent conclusions of Dunja Mijatović, Council of Europe Commissioner for Human Rights, who stated in March 2022 that: "The digital dimension of VAWG remains insufficiently addressed" and that common shortcomings in most domestic laws is that the issue "is only partially tackled through the lens of children’s rights and Internet safety." \[11\] We would advise instead that the Bill adopts an overarching human rights and equalities framework,\[12\] which names VAWG, along with gender and other protected characteristics. ***VAWG Code of Practice***
chunks.display_text: 15.Furthermore, the Bill wrongly adopts a gender-neutral approach. Risk assessments will not be explicitly required to reflect the gendered nature of VAWG in the same way that there is in the Home Office’s Tackling VAWG strategy or its Domestic Abuse Plan. This is because the risk assessment duties for adults and children never actually name gender as a factor, nor any protected characteristics specifically - only "content that particularly affects individuals with a certain characteristic or members of a certain group." This is not defined,\[9\] and we are not confident that tech companies will make suitable judgements regarding how this should be interpreted, particularly given concerns about their lack of diversity and VAWG expertise.\[10\] A gender-neutral approach to tackling online VAWG is against **t** he advice of the gold-standard Istanbul Convention, to which the UK is a signatory and has recently agreed to ratify. Our concerns also chime with the recent conclusions of Dunja Mijatović, Council of Europe Commissioner for Human Rights, who stated in March 2022 that: "The digital dimension of VAWG remains insufficiently addressed" and that common shortcomings in most domestic laws is that the issue "is only partially tackled through the lens of children’s rights and Internet safety." \[11\] We would advise instead that the Bill adopts an overarching human rights and equalities framework,\[12\] which names VAWG, along with gender and other protected characteristics. ***VAWG Code of Practice***

chunks.text: most domestic laws is that the issue "is only partially tackled through the lens of children’s rights and Internet safety." \[11\] We would advise instead that the Bill adopts an overarching human rights and equalities framework,\[12\] which names VAWG, along with gender and other protected characteristics. ***VAWG Code of Practice*** 16.In order to address the shortcomings outlined above, EVAW has worked with Glitch, Refuge, NSPCC, 5Rights, Carnegie UK and Professors Lorna Woods and Clare McGlynn to produce a VAWG Code of Practice which we recommend the Bill be amended to include. This document, produced in consultation with industry itself, provides comprehensive guidelines to assist platforms in scrutinising their decision-making around the design and operation of their systems and the extent to which they are conducive to VAWG as a whole, not only the specific content listed in Schedule 7. This cross-cutting code encourages platforms to consider how to prevent VAWG across the three separate buckets of ‘illegal content’, ‘content harmful to children’ and ‘content harmful to adults’. Specifically, it contains guidance on areas such as: risk assessments, safety by design, victim support, education and training for example. It informs platforms to consider how specific decision choices such as recommender algorithms or autoplay functions and pop-ups for example, might play a role in enabling and/or amplifying VAWG. The Code of Practice is available here, and you can watch its launch event, co-chaired by Maria Miller and Rt Hon Baroness Morgan of Cotes here. ***Media literacy***
chunks.display_text: 16.In order to address the shortcomings outlined above, EVAW has worked with Glitch, Refuge, NSPCC, 5Rights, Carnegie UK and Professors Lorna Woods and Clare McGlynn to produce a VAWG Code of Practice which we recommend the Bill be amended to include. This document, produced in consultation with industry itself, provides comprehensive guidelines to assist platforms in scrutinising their decision-making around the design and operation of their systems and the extent to which they are conducive to VAWG as a whole, not only the specific content listed in Schedule 7. This cross-cutting code encourages platforms to consider how to prevent VAWG across the three separate buckets of ‘illegal content’, ‘content harmful to children’ and ‘content harmful to adults’. Specifically, it contains guidance on areas such as: risk assessments, safety by design, victim support, education and training for example. It informs platforms to consider how specific decision choices such as recommender algorithms or autoplay functions and pop-ups for example, might play a role in enabling and/or amplifying VAWG. The Code of Practice is available here, and you can watch its launch event, co-chaired by Maria Miller and Rt Hon Baroness Morgan of Cotes here. ***Media literacy***

chunks.text: decision choices such as recommender algorithms or autoplay functions and pop-ups for example, might play a role in enabling and/or amplifying VAWG. The Code of Practice is available here, and you can watch its launch event, co-chaired by Maria Miller and Rt Hon Baroness Morgan of Cotes here. ***Media literacy*** 17.In order to better address online VAWG, the Bill should be amended to ensure greater provisions for media literacy. EVAW is concerned that otherwise, there is a risk that this area follows a similar trajectory of Relationships and Sex Education in terms of inadequate funding and resources for delivery in a manner which is transformative and effective. The draft Bill proposed an amendment to section 11 of the Communications Act 2003 (duty to promote media literacy), which referenced work around "the impact that such material may have (for example, the impact on the behaviour of those who receive it"**.**This clause was important in empowering OFCOM to include the consequences of online abuse in the scope of its media literacy campaigns. However, in the current version, the emphasis is instead on ‘users’ media literacy and safe use of the service’. We see this as a regressive step, as it does not communicate the collective responsibility we have online, nor the need for increased awareness that behaviour and conduct online, including online VAWG, has real and long-lasting consequences. ***Funding for specialist VAWG services***
chunks.display_text: 17.In order to better address online VAWG, the Bill should be amended to ensure greater provisions for media literacy. EVAW is concerned that otherwise, there is a risk that this area follows a similar trajectory of Relationships and Sex Education in terms of inadequate funding and resources for delivery in a manner which is transformative and effective. The draft Bill proposed an amendment to section 11 of the Communications Act 2003 (duty to promote media literacy), which referenced work around "the impact that such material may have (for example, the impact on the behaviour of those who receive it"**.**This clause was important in empowering OFCOM to include the consequences of online abuse in the scope of its media literacy campaigns. However, in the current version, the emphasis is instead on ‘users’ media literacy and safe use of the service’. We see this as a regressive step, as it does not communicate the collective responsibility we have online, nor the need for increased awareness that behaviour and conduct online, including online VAWG, has real and long-lasting consequences. ***Funding for specialist VAWG services***

chunks.text: media literacy and safe use of the service’. We see this as a regressive step, as it does not communicate the collective responsibility we have online, nor the need for increased awareness that behaviour and conduct online, including online VAWG, has real and long-lasting consequences. ***Funding for specialist VAWG services*** 18.Women and girls subject to abuse online often rely on specialist VAWG services for support, however the Bill does not provide any additional resources for such services. Such services are currently severely underfunded and unable to respond to the huge levels of demand they currently face. Between April 2020 and May 2021, Refuge saw on average a 97% increase in the number of complex tech abuse cases requiring specialist tech support compared to the first three months of 2020. Financial provision for support for victims of online harms must be provided in order that they are able to receive independent, specialist and trauma informed support and advocacy from organisations that are experts in responding to VAWG and working with online harms. EVAW is in favour of the ‘polluter pays’ principle which suggests that the platforms enabling these harms should pay towards remediation. The Bill should be amended to ringfence at least 10% of the Digital Services Tax to specialist support services, including led by and for specialist VAWG services. **User empowerment tools**
chunks.display_text: 18.Women and girls subject to abuse online often rely on specialist VAWG services for support, however the Bill does not provide any additional resources for such services. Such services are currently severely underfunded and unable to respond to the huge levels of demand they currently face. Between April 2020 and May 2021, Refuge saw on average a 97% increase in the number of complex tech abuse cases requiring specialist tech support compared to the first three months of 2020. Financial provision for support for victims of online harms must be provided in order that they are able to receive independent, specialist and trauma informed support and advocacy from organisations that are experts in responding to VAWG and working with online harms. EVAW is in favour of the ‘polluter pays’ principle which suggests that the platforms enabling these harms should pay towards remediation. The Bill should be amended to ringfence at least 10% of the Digital Services Tax to specialist support services, including led by and for specialist VAWG services. **User empowerment tools**

chunks.text: is in favour of the ‘polluter pays’ principle which suggests that the platforms enabling these harms should pay towards remediation. The Bill should be amended to ringfence at least 10% of the Digital Services Tax to specialist support services, including led by and for specialist VAWG services. **User empowerment tools** 19.Government has highlighted ‘user empowerment tools’ as one means of addressing VAWG,\[13\] however we would caution against a reliance on anonymity and verification as a means of upholding women and girls rights. Survivors of gender-based violence are only one cohort who may rely on anonymity online for their safety. We are averse to the creation of a tiered experience of the internet, where the right to participate online freely is largely contingent on having the freedom or privilege to verify a person’s identity. We are also concerned that this might shift responsibility for addressing VAWG from the tech companies themselves. The onus should be on tech companies to be accountable for the decisions they make and the extent to which they can create enabling conditions for VAWG, over and above women and girls having to rely on filtering their online experience as a route to avoiding harm. --- \[1\] Amnesty International UK (2017) Online abuse of women widespread in UK https://www.amnesty.org.uk/online-abuse-women-widespread \[2\] NSPCC found that 4 in 5 sexual communication with a child offences were committed against girls: https://www.nspcc.org.uk/about-us/news-opinion/2021/online-grooming-crimes-girls/ T he Independent Inquiry into Child Sexual Abuse found that girls are more likely than boys to be victims of online-facilitated child sexual abuse. \[3\] Amnesty International: Troll patrol findings: https://decoders.amnesty.org/projects/troll-patrol/findings \[4\] End Violence Against Women Coalition and Glitch (2020) The Ripple Effect: Covid-19 and the Epidemic of Online Abuse: https://www.endviolenceagainstwomen.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/Glitch-and-EVAW-The-Ripple-Effect-Online-abuse-during-COVID-19-Sept-2020.pdf \[5\] Women’s Aid (2021) Research on Online Domestic Abuse https://www.womensaid.org.uk/information-support/what-is-domestic-abuse/onlinesafety/ \[5\] \[6\] https://committees.parliament.uk/publications/8608/documents/86960/default/ \[7\] https://www.coe.int/en/web/istanbul-convention/basic-texts \[8\] https://hansard.parliament.uk/Commons/2022-05-26/debates/a8f25ba3-fcfa-460b-8287-055606dcc344/details \[8\]

Survivors of gender-based violence are only one cohort who may rely on anonymity online for their safety. We are averse to the creation of a tiered experience of the internet, where the right to participate online freely is largely contingent on having the freedom or privilege to verify a person’s identity. We are also concerned that this might shift responsibility for addressing VAWG from the tech companies themselves. The onus should be on tech companies to be accountable for the decisions they make and the extent to which they can create enabling conditions for VAWG, over and above women and girls having to rely on filtering their online experience as a route to avoiding harm. --- \[1\] Amnesty International UK (2017) Online abuse of women widespread in UK https://www.amnesty.org.uk/online-abuse-women-widespread \[2\] NSPCC found that 4 in 5 sexual communication with a child offences were committed against girls: https://www.nspcc.org.uk/about-us/news-opinion/2021/online-grooming-crimes-girls/ T he Independent Inquiry into Child Sexual Abuse found that girls are more likely than boys to be victims of online-facilitated child sexual abuse. \[3\] Amnesty International: Troll patrol findings: https://decoders.amnesty.org/projects/troll-patrol/findings \[4\] End Violence Against Women Coalition and Glitch (2020) The Ripple Effect: Covid-19 and the Epidemic of Online Abuse: https://www.endviolenceagainstwomen.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/Glitch-and-EVAW-The-Ripple-Effect-Online-abuse-during-COVID-19-Sept-2020.pdf \[5\] Women’s Aid (2021) Research on Online Domestic Abuse https://www.womensaid.org.uk/information-support/what-is-domestic-abuse/onlinesafety/ \[5\] \[6\] https://committees.parliament.uk/publications/8608/documents/86960/default/ \[7\] https://www.coe.int/en/web/istanbul-convention/basic-texts \[8\] https://hansard.parliament.uk/Commons/2022-05-26/debates/a8f25ba3-fcfa-460b-8287-055606dcc344/details \[8\]

chunks.text: of online-facilitated child sexual abuse. \[3\] Amnesty International: Troll patrol findings: https://decoders.amnesty.org/projects/troll-patrol/findings \[4\] End Violence Against Women Coalition and Glitch (2020) The Ripple Effect: Covid-19 and the Epidemic of Online Abuse: https://www.endviolenceagainstwomen.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/Glitch-and-EVAW-The-Ripple-Effect-Online-abuse-during-COVID-19-Sept-2020.pdf \[5\] Women’s Aid (2021) Research on Online Domestic Abuse https://www.womensaid.org.uk/information-support/what-is-domestic-abuse/onlinesafety/ \[5\] \[6\] https://committees.parliament.uk/publications/8608/documents/86960/default/ \[7\] https://www.coe.int/en/web/istanbul-convention/basic-texts \[8\] https://hansard.parliament.uk/Commons/2022-05-26/debates/a8f25ba3-fcfa-460b-8287-055606dcc344/details \[8\] \[9\] This description also does not appear to account for individuals who are members of multiple groups \[10\] United Nations General Assembly, Report of the Special Rapporteur on the promotion and protection of the right to freedom of opinion and expression, Irene Khan, 30 July 2021 said: "The setting of rules by social media platforms through community guidelines and moderation by algorithms is not objective. It reflects the biases and worldviews of the rule-setters, who tend to be typically from the specific sociocultural context of Silicon Valley: racially monochromatic and economically elite. The gender bias evident in content moderation reinforces the argument for companies to base their content moderation on international human rights standards. \[11\] https://www.coe.int/en/web/commissioner/-/no-space-for-violence-against-women-and-girls-in-the-digital-world \[12\] To note, EVAW would also ask the Committee to consider the potential adverse impact of plans to overhaul the Human Rights Act on the implementation of the Online Safety Bill. \[13\] https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/online-safety-bill-supporting-documents/online-safety-bill-factsheet#what-the-bill-does-to-protect-women \[13\] \[13\] \[13\] \[13\] \[13\]
